title: 1 Chronicles 7
subtitle: Issachar, Benjamin, Naphtali, Manasseh, Ephraim, Asher

sections.header: Verses 1–19 — Issachar, Benjamin, Naphtali, Western Manasseh

refs.note: Jacob’s blessing on Issachar: “a rawboned donkey lying down among the sheep pens.” The Chronicler provides the military counterpoint: 87,000 warriors.

notes.note: The military census figures remind us that Israel was a real nation with real armies. The genealogies are not abstract theology but records of people who lived, fought, and died. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: Naphtali’s single-verse genealogy lists Jahziel, Guni, Jezer, and Shallum — matching Gen 46:24 exactly. The Chronicler simply reproduces the Genesis tradition without expansion. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: Six tribes are covered in compressed genealogies. The Chronicler’s interest varies — Issachar gets military census data (87,000 fighting men), Benjamin is listed briefly (expanded in chapter 8), Naphtali gets a single verse, and western Manasseh receives moderate treatment. The uneven coverage reflects the Chronicler’s priorities: tribes relevant to the post-exilic community (Judah, Levi, Benjamin) get detailed treatment; northern tribes get summaries.

heb.paragraph: The Hebrew tôlĕḏôt (generations) is a structuring device that traces the thread of God's purpose through family lines. From Genesis to Chronicles, genealogies are not mere lists but theological arguments: they demonstrate continuity, establish legitimacy, and show that God's covenant promises pass from generation to generation.

notes.note: The Chronicler records this history not merely as political narrative but as theological instruction. Every event demonstrates God's sovereign governance of His people and His unwavering faithfulness to the Davidic covenant. The believing reader is meant to see in these events the pattern of God's dealings with His church in every age.

The Chronicler's perspective consistently emphasizes God's faithfulness to His covenant promises despite Israel's unfaithfulness. This dual theme — human failure and divine grace — is the heartbeat of all Scripture and finds its ultimate resolution in the cross of Christ, where God's justice and mercy meet perfectly (Rom 3:25-26).

The Chronicler wrote for a post-exilic community needing to understand their identity and hope. His message remains vital: God's promises to David are irrevocable, worship is the center of covenant life, and faithfulness — not circumstances — determines blessing. Believers today can take the same comfort: our God is faithful even when we are not (2 Tim 2:13).

sections.header: Verses 20–40 — Ephraim (Including the Mourning Episode); Asher

refs.note: Jacob blessed Ephraim over Manasseh, giving the younger son precedence. The Chronicler’s Ephraim genealogy includes both blessing and grief.

notes.note: Ephraim’s mourning teaches that even patriarchs are not immune to grief. The community that gathers to comfort him models the response God desires: shared sorrow, not stoic isolation. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: bĕrîʿāh is explained as “in misfortune” (bĕrāʿāh). The folk etymology connects the name to the family’s experience of loss, following the biblical naming pattern where names encode circumstances. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: Ephraim’s genealogy includes a unique mourning episode: men of Gath killed Ezer and Elead (Ephraim’s sons) when they went down to seize livestock. “Their father Ephraim mourned for them many days, and his relatives came to comfort him” (v.22). Then he had another son named Beriah (“because misfortune had come to his family”). This brief narrative — grief, naming, and recovery — humanises the genealogy. The chapter closes with Asher’s genealogy, emphasising military strength (26,000 warriors).

heb.paragraph: The Hebrew qōḏeš (holiness) denotes separation for divine purpose. In the context of temple worship, it marks the distinction between common and sacred — a boundary that defines Israel's entire liturgical life. The Chronicler's emphasis on holiness reflects his concern for proper worship as the foundation of national life.

notes.note: The Chronicler records this history not merely as political narrative but as theological instruction. Every event demonstrates God's sovereign governance of His people and His unwavering faithfulness to the Davidic covenant. The believing reader is meant to see in these events the pattern of God's dealings with His church in every age.

The Chronicler's perspective consistently emphasizes God's faithfulness to His covenant promises despite Israel's unfaithfulness. This dual theme — human failure and divine grace — is the heartbeat of all Scripture and finds its ultimate resolution in the cross of Christ, where God's justice and mercy meet perfectly (Rom 3:25-26).

The Chronicler wrote for a post-exilic community needing to understand their identity and hope. His message remains vital: God's promises to David are irrevocable, worship is the center of covenant life, and faithfulness — not circumstances — determines blessing. Believers today can take the same comfort: our God is faithful even when we are not (2 Tim 2:13).

ppl.text: The uneven coverage reflects the Chronicler’s priorities: tribes relevant to the post-exilic community (Judah, Levi, Benjamin) get detailed treatment…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVTheir genealogical record listed the heads of families and 20,200 fighting men.ESVAnd their enrollment by genealogies, according to their generations, as heads of their fathers\u2019 houses, mighty warriors, was 20,200.NIVAccording to their family genealogy, they had 36,000 men ready for battle, for they had many wives and children.ESVAnd along with them, by their generations, according to their fathers\u2019 houses, were units of the army for war, 36,000, for they had many wives and sons.

themes.note: Issachar, Benjamin, Naphtali, Manasseh, Ephraim, Asher

coaching.tip: The northern tribes receive briefer genealogies but they are included. The Chronicler's vision of 'all Israel' encompasses tribes that no longer exist as political units. The genealogical register is an act of faith — claiming a unity that history has shattered.

prayer_prompt: These tribal genealogies preserved identity through displacement. Consider asking God about the identity markers you carry — which come from Him and which you've constructed.
